title: Apple and blackberry pie
subtitle: Deep-dish double-crust shortcrust pie with a sharp apple and blackberry filling.
excerpt: Apple and blackberry pie is a double-crust shortcrust pastry pie filled with sliced cooking apples and fresh or frozen blackberries sweetened with sugar and spiced with cinnamon. The pastry is made by the rub-in method, which is quicker than a food processor and produces a short, crumbly result. The filling needs no pre-cooking.
sourceNotes: Apple pie has been a British baking staple since at least the fourteenth century, when recipes appear in medieval manuscripts. The double-crust format enclosing a sweetened fruit filling is the form that became standard in British household cookbooks from the seventeenth century onwards. Blackberries paired with apple reflect the British seasonal harvest: both ripen at the same time in September and October, and the sharpness of wild blackberries balances the tartness of cooking apples. This recipe follows the standard British shortcrust method.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze the unbaked assembled pie. Cook from frozen at 180°C fan for 55 to 60 minutes, covering with foil if the pastry browns before the filling is hot.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: The pastry can be made up to two days ahead and refrigerated, wrapped. The assembled pie can be refrigerated unbaked for up to 24 hours.

recipeTools.notes: 23 cm pie dish, about 5 cm deep.

glossaryTerms.definition: A crumbly, tender pastry made by rubbing cold fat into flour until the mixture resembles breadcrumbs, then binding with a little cold water. The ratio of fat to flour is roughly half fat to flour by weight. Kept cold throughout, it produces a pastry that is short (crumbles) rather than chewy.

content.text: is keeping everything cold. Cold fat, cold water, cold hands. Work quickly and stop mixing as soon as the dough holds together. Overworking develops gluten and makes the pastry tough rather than short.

items.prepNote: Bramley or other cooking apple, peeled, cored, sliced
fresh or frozen (no need to defrost)
adjust to taste depending on apple tartness

content.text: Sift {{plain-flour}}, {{icing-sugar}}, and {{sea-salt-fine}} into a large bowl. Rub in the cold {{unsalted-butter}} until the mixture looks like rough breadcrumbs. Add {{eggs}} (just enough to bind; start with the beaten egg and use cold water to finish if needed) and mix to a firm dough. Divide in two, one slightly larger than the other. Flatten into discs, wrap, and refrigerate for 30 minutes.
Heat the oven to 190°C fan (210°C conventional). Place a baking tray in the oven to heat.

Roll the larger pastry disc on a floured surface to a circle about 3 mm thick and large enough to line the pie dish with overhang. Lay it in and press into the base and sides. Fill with the fruit mixture, mounding it slightly in the centre.

Roll the second disc to the same thickness. Brush the pastry rim with beaten egg, lay the lid on top, and press the edges together. Trim the excess and crimp. Cut a small cross in the centre to let steam escape. Brush the top with beaten egg and scatter with caster sugar if using.
Place on the hot baking tray in the oven. Bake for 40 to 45 minutes until deep golden all over. Cover loosely with foil if the top colours too quickly. Leave to rest for 10 minutes before cutting.

attrs.heading: Troubleshooting
attrs.intro: Pastry problems are nearly all about temperature and handling.

items.cause: Pie placed on a cold tray, or filling too wet.
items.fix: Always bake on a preheated metal tray to crisp the base from the start. The cornflour absorbs juice from the fruit; without it the base will likely be wet.

items.cause: Overstretched when lining the dish, or not chilled before baking.
items.fix: Press the pastry gently into the dish rather than stretching it. If it looks very elastic, refrigerate for a further 15 minutes before filling.
